words.word: hit the nail on the head
words.definition: to say or do exactly the right thing
example_sentences: She hit the nail on the head with her analysis.
You've hit the nail on the head — that's exactly the problem.
His report really hit the nail on the head.
words.pronunciation_tip: HIT the NAYL on the HED.
words.common_mistake: Don't translate literally — this means to identify or say something exactly right.
words.collocations: ['really hit the nail on the head', 'you hit the nail on the head', 'hit the nail on the head with']

words.definition: to endure something painful or difficult
example_sentences: He bit the bullet and went to the dentist.
She bit the bullet and had the difficult conversation.
Sometimes you just have to bite the bullet.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he accepted the pain' — say 'he bit the bullet'.
words.collocations: ['bite the bullet and', 'just bite the bullet', 'have to bite the bullet']

words.definition: to permanently damage a relationship or opportunity
example_sentences: Don't burn your bridges — you might need them later.
She left the company without burning any bridges.
He burned his bridges by speaking badly of his boss.
words.common_mistake: Don't translate literally — means to destroy future possibilities by doing something irreversible.
words.collocations: ['burn your bridges', 'don't burn bridges', 'burn bridges with']

words.word: the ball is in your court
words.definition: it is your turn to take action or make a decision
example_sentences: I've made my offer — the ball is in your court now.
She's done all she can. The ball is in his court.
The ball is in your court — what do you decide?
words.pronunciation_tip: the BAWL is in your KAWT.
words.common_mistake: Don't translate literally — means it's your turn to decide or act.
words.collocations: ['the ball is in your court', 'ball is in their court', 'now the ball is in your court']

words.definition: to miss an opportunity because you acted too late
example_sentences: He missed the boat on investing in crypto early on.
Don't miss the boat — register before Friday!
She missed the boat on the promotion because she waited too long.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he lost the chance' always — say 'he missed the boat'.
words.collocations: ['miss the boat on', 'don't miss the boat', 'missed the boat']

words.definition: to tell someone something that is not true as a joke
example_sentences: Are you pulling my leg? That can't be real!
He was just pulling her leg — he didn't mean it seriously.
I thought he was pulling my leg but he was serious.
words.common_mistake: Don't translate literally — means to joke or tease someone.
words.collocations: ['pull my leg', 'just pulling your leg', 'is he pulling your leg']

words.word: cost an arm and a leg
words.definition: to be very expensive
example_sentences: That holiday cost an arm and a leg.
Childcare in the city centre costs an arm and a leg.
Repairs on old cars always cost an arm and a leg.
words.pronunciation_tip: KOST un ARM and a LEG.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'it was very expensive' always — say 'it cost an arm and a leg'.
words.collocations: ['cost an arm and a leg', 'really costs an arm and a leg', 'that costs an arm and a leg']

words.definition: feeling slightly ill or unwell
example_sentences: I'm feeling a bit under the weather today.
She's been under the weather all week — probably a cold.
He stayed home because he was feeling under the weather.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I am a bit sick' — say 'I'm feeling a bit under the weather'.
words.collocations: ['feeling under the weather', 'a bit under the weather', 'been under the weather']

words.definition: having the same understanding or agreement about something
example_sentences: Before we start, let's make sure we're all on the same page.
Are we on the same page about the deadline?
They spent an hour getting on the same page.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'we all understand each other' — say 'we're on the same page'.
words.collocations: ['on the same page', 'get on the same page', 'make sure we're on the same page']

words.definition: to start something quickly and energetically from the beginning
example_sentences: She really hit the ground running in her new role.
We need someone who can hit the ground running from day one.
He hit the ground running and delivered results in week one.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she started well quickly' — say 'she hit the ground running'.
words.collocations: ['hit the ground running', 'need to hit the ground running', 'really hit the ground running']

words.definition: to start a process or activity
example_sentences: Let's get the ball rolling with a quick introduction.
She got the ball rolling by suggesting the first topic.
We need someone to get the ball rolling on this project.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'let's start' every time — say 'let's get the ball rolling'.
words.collocations: ['get the ball rolling', 'let's get the ball rolling', 'start to get the ball rolling']

words.definition: only a small visible part of a much larger problem
example_sentences: This complaint is just the tip of the iceberg.
The financial problems we can see are only the tip of the iceberg.
What you've found so far is just the tip of the iceberg.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a small part of a bigger problem' every time — say 'the tip of the iceberg'.
words.collocations: ['just the tip of the iceberg', 'only the tip of the iceberg', 'tip of the iceberg of']

words.definition: to admit defeat and give up
example_sentences: After three failed attempts, he finally threw in the towel.
Don't throw in the towel — you can still win this.
She refused to throw in the towel despite all the setbacks.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he gave up' always — say 'he threw in the towel'.
words.collocations: ['throw in the towel', 'don't throw in the towel', 'finally threw in the towel']

words.definition: to avoid talking directly about the main point
example_sentences: Stop beating around the bush and tell me what happened.
He always beats around the bush instead of being direct.
She appreciated that he didn't beat around the bush.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he doesn't say things directly' — say 'he beats around the bush'.
words.collocations: ['stop beating around the bush', 'tend to beat around the bush', 'beating around the bush']

words.definition: very rarely
example_sentences: He visits his family once in a blue moon.
I eat fast food once in a blue moon.
Once in a blue moon, everything goes perfectly.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'very rarely' every time — say 'once in a blue moon'.
words.collocations: ['once in a blue moon', 'happens once in a blue moon', 'only once in a blue moon']

words.definition: having to start again from the beginning
example_sentences: The deal fell through, so we're back to square one.
The failed prototype put them back to square one.
After the rejection, she was back to square one.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'we have to start again' — say 'we're back to square one'.
words.collocations: ['back to square one', 'send back to square one', 'back at square one']

words.definition: to become nervous and hesitant about something you planned to do
example_sentences: He got cold feet before the wedding and nearly didn't show up.
She got cold feet before signing the contract.
Don't get cold feet now — you've worked so hard.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he became nervous' — say 'he got cold feet'.
words.collocations: ['get cold feet', 'getting cold feet', 'got cold feet about']

words.word: let the cat out of the bag
words.definition: to accidentally reveal a secret
example_sentences: She let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.
He let the cat out of the bag too early.
Who let the cat out of the bag?
words.pronunciation_tip: let the KAT owt of the BAG.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she told the secret accidentally' — say 'she let the cat out of the bag'.
words.collocations: ['let the cat out of the bag', 'accidentally let the cat out', 'who let the cat out of the bag']

words.word: take it with a grain of salt
words.definition: to not completely believe something because it may be exaggerated
example_sentences: Take everything he says with a grain of salt.
I'd take that review with a grain of salt — it seems biased.
She took the good news with a grain of salt.
words.pronunciation_tip: TAYK it with a GRAYN of SAWLT.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'don't believe it completely' — say 'take it with a grain of salt'.
words.collocations: ['take with a grain of salt', 'take what they say with a grain of salt', 'best taken with a grain of salt']

words.definition: to agree with someone completely
example_sentences: They don't always see eye to eye on management style.
Finally, we see eye to eye on the strategy.
She and her boss rarely see eye to eye.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'they agree' every time — say 'they see eye to eye'.
words.collocations: ['see eye to eye on', 'don't always see eye to eye', 'rarely see eye to eye']
